base_method_text:
{"arxiv_id":"2306.11922v1","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2306.11922v1","title":"No Wrong Turns: The Simple Geometry Of Neural Networks Optimization
  Paths","authors":["Charles Guille-Escuret","Hiroki Naganuma","Kilian Fatras","Ioannis Mitliagkas"],"published_date":"2023-06-20T22:10:40Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Understanding the optimization dynamics of neural networks is necessary for
closing the gap between theory and practice. Stochastic first-order
optimization algorithms are known to efficiently locate favorable minima in
deep neural networks. This efficiency, however, contrasts with the non-convex
and seemingly complex structure of neural loss landscapes. In this study, we
delve into the fundamental geometric properties of sampled gradients along
optimization paths. We focus on two key quantities, which appear in the
restricted secant inequality and error bound. Both hold high significance for
first-order optimization. Our analysis reveals that these quantities exhibit
predictable, consistent behavior throughout training, despite the stochasticity
induced by sampling minibatches. Our findings suggest that not only do
optimization trajectories never encounter significant obstacles, but they also
maintain stable dynamics during the majority of training. These observed
properties are sufficiently expressive to theoretically guarantee linear
convergence and prescribe learning rate schedules mirroring empirical
practices. We conduct our experiments on image classification, semantic
segmentation and language modeling across different batch sizes, network
architectures, datasets, optimizers, and initialization seeds. We discuss the
impact of each factor. Our work provides novel insights into the properties of
neural network loss functions, and opens the door to theoretical frameworks
more relevant to prevalent practice.","github_url":"https://github.com/Hiroki11x/LossLandscapeGeometry","main_contributions":"The paper investigates the geometric properties of neural network loss landscapes along optimization paths, focusing on the Restricted Secant Inequality (RSI) and Error Bound (EB). It empirically demonstrates that these quantities exhibit stable and predictable behavior during training, suggesting that optimization trajectories do not encounter significant obstacles and maintain stable dynamics. The study also shows how these properties can inform learning rate schedule prescriptions.","methodology":"The study uses an experimental procedure to examine the geometry of optimization paths, calculating RSI and EB using sampled gradients, current iterates, and the final iterate of the optimization sequence. The cosine similarity between the negative stochastic gradient and the direction to the final iterate is analyzed. The paper assumes almost-everywhere differentiability, but not smoothness.","experimental_setup":"Experiments were conducted on image classification (CIFAR-10, ImageNet), semantic segmentation (Vaihingen), and language modeling (WikiText-2) tasks. Various network architectures (ResNet, Transformer, U-Net, SegNet), batch sizes, optimizers (SGD, Adam), and initialization seeds were tested. The final iterate of the optimization sequence is used as a reference point for calculating RSI and EB.","limitations":"A primary limitation is the correlation between the final iterate used as a reference point and the optimization sequence, which can induce bias, especially in the concluding epochs. The paper acknowledges that RSI and EB might represent local properties dependent on the initialization region. Also, the paper focuses on almost-everywhere differentiable loss functions, potentially overlooking non-differentiable scenarios.","future_research_directions":"Future research could explore additional geometric properties beyond RSI and EB that contribute to the success of neural networks. The paper suggests investigating how momentum exploits properties not captured by RSI and EB. Furthermore, there is a call to develop theoretical results tailored to deep learning based on the observed geometric properties, or for designing optimization algorithms that leverage the geometry of empirical objective functions."}
add_method_texts:
{"arxiv_id":"2306.08125v2","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2306.08125v2","title":"Implicit Compressibility of Overparametrized Neural Networks Trained
  with Heavy-Tailed SGD","authors":["Yijun Wan","Melih Barsbey","Abdellatif Zaidi","Umut Simsekli"],"published_date":"2023-06-13T20:37:02Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Neural network compression has been an increasingly important subject, not
only due to its practical relevance, but also due to its theoretical
implications, as there is an explicit connection between compressibility and
generalization error. Recent studies have shown that the choice of the
hyperparameters of stochastic gradient descent (SGD) can have an effect on the
compressibility of the learned parameter vector. These results, however, rely
on unverifiable assumptions and the resulting theory does not provide a
practical guideline due to its implicitness. In this study, we propose a simple
modification for SGD, such that the outputs of the algorithm will be provably
compressible without making any nontrivial assumptions. We consider a
one-hidden-layer neural network trained with SGD, and show that if we inject
additive heavy-tailed noise to the iterates at each iteration, for any
compression rate, there exists a level of overparametrization such that the
output of the algorithm will be compressible with high probability. To achieve
this result, we make two main technical contributions: (i) we prove a
'propagation of chaos' result for a class of heavy-tailed stochastic
differential equations, and (ii) we derive error estimates for their Euler
discretization. Our experiments suggest that the proposed approach not only
achieves increased compressibility with various models and datasets, but also
leads to robust test performance under pruning, even in more realistic
architectures that lie beyond our theoretical setting.","github_url":"https://github.com/mbarsbey/implicit-compressibility","main_contributions":"The paper proposes a modification to Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) by injecting additive heavy-tailed noise to the iterates, demonstrating that this provably leads to compressible neural networks without making strong assumptions. The research shows that overparametrization combined with heavy-tailed noise makes the weight matrix compressible with high probability, and provides theoretical guarantees for compressibility in single-hidden-layer neural networks.","methodology":"The methodology involves analyzing a one-hidden-layer neural network trained with SGD and perturbed by heavy-tailed noise. It uses mean-field limits to show that, in the limit, columns of the weight matrix become independent and identically distributed with a heavy-tailed distribution. The proofs rely on (i) a "propagation of chaos" result for heavy-tailed stochastic differential equations (SDEs), and (ii) error estimates for Euler discretization of these SDEs. The approach leverages results from Gribonval et al. (2012) to establish a high-probability compression bound.","experimental_setup":"The approach is validated using various datasets, including ECG5000, MNIST, CIFAR10, and CIFAR100. Experiments are conducted on single and multi-layer fully connected neural networks (FCNs) and convolutional neural networks (CNNs). The performance is evaluated based on training and test accuracy before and after pruning, and the pruning ratio is used to measure compressibility. SGD's step size and noise level are tuned to ensure convergence while approximating continuous dynamics.","limitations":"The theoretical results are primarily focused on single-hidden-layer mean-field networks, which is a simplification compared to more sophisticated architectures used in practice. The paper acknowledges that generalizing the theoretical results to more complex architectures and adaptive optimizers would be beneficial. The study also points out a trade-off between compressibility and optimization performance due to the injected heavy-tailed noise.","future_research_directions":"The paper suggests several future research directions, including: (i) generalizing the theoretical results to more sophisticated architectures, (ii) extending the results to adaptive optimization schemes, and (iii) gaining an improved understanding of the interplay between data distribution, learning rate, noise type, and architecture in producing compressibility."}
